title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description:
There is an integer array `nums` sorted in ascending order (with distinct values) that has been rotated at an unknown pivot. Given the array `nums` and an integer `target`, return the index of `target` if it is in `nums`, or `-1` if it is not.

You must write an algorithm with **O(log n)** runtime complexity.

**Key Insight:** Even though the array is rotated, one half is always sorted. Determine which half is sorted, check if the target lies in that half, and binary search accordingly.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 5000`
- All values in `nums` are unique.
- `nums` is an ascending array rotated at some pivot.

examples.explanation: Target 3 is not in the array.
Single element array, target not found.

editorial: Binary search with modified condition. At each step, determine which half is sorted: if nums[left]<=nums[mid], left half is sorted. Check if target is in [nums[left], nums[mid]]; if yes search left, else search right. Otherwise right half is sorted; check if target in (nums[mid], nums[right]]; if yes search right, else search left. O(log n).

rexHints.level1: You can still use binary search — even in a rotated array, at least one half is always fully sorted.
rexHints.level2: At each midpoint, figure out which half is sorted (compare nums[left] with nums[mid]). Then check if the target falls within the sorted half's range to decide which side to search.
rexHints.level3: left=0,right=n-1. While left<=right: mid=(left+right)/2. If nums[mid]==target return mid. If nums[left]<=nums[mid]: if nums[left]<=target<nums[mid] right=mid-1 else left=mid+1. Else: if nums[mid]<target<=nums[right] left=mid+1 else right=mid-1. Return -1.
